title: Surge tokens should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Surge authentication token detected. Surge.sh uses UUID-format authentication tokens for CLI operations and API access. These standalone tokens provide the same deployment capabilities as email:token credential pairs, enabling: publishing and updating static websites to Surge domains, overwriting production deployments with malicious content, deleting published projects and domains from your account, registering and claiming new domains under your Surge account, modifying DNS configurations and domain settings, accessing deployment history and project metadata, consuming deployment quota and bandwidth allocation, and abusing infrastructure for hosting unauthorized content. Exposed Surge tokens allow attackers to: deploy sophisticated phishing sites on your legitimate domains, inject malicious JavaScript for credential harvesting or cryptomining, replace production sites with defacement or propaganda content, use domains for malware distribution or command-and-control infrastructure, exhaust deployment limits preventing legitimate updates, steal SEO rankings by redirecting traffic to malicious sites, and damage brand reputation through unauthorized deployments. Unlike email:token pairs which require both components, standalone tokens provide immediate authentication capability. Surge tokens are permanent by default and remain valid until explicitly revoked through 'surge logout' or account dashboard. Token exposure commonly occurs through: committing configuration files to version control (.npmrc .yarnrc surge.json), hardcoding in deployment scripts and CI/CD pipelines, logging during debug operations or error handling, exposing in environment variable dumps or system information, storing in plaintext configuration management tools, sharing via unsecured communication channels, and leaving in public GitHub gists or pastebin services. The security impact is severe as Surge hosts production websites and applications accessed by end users, making credential exposure directly exploitable for: website defacement affecting brand reputation and customer trust, phishing attacks impersonating your organization to steal user credentials, malware distribution infecting visitor systems through compromised deployments, SEO poisoning redirecting organic traffic to malicious destinations, regulatory compliance violations (GDPR CCPA HIPAA) due to security breaches, financial losses from incident response and legal liabilities, and supply chain attacks if Surge domains serve assets for third-party applications. Immediate remediation required: revoke exposed token immediately using 'surge logout' command or dashboard token management (tokens remain valid even if repository made private), audit all Surge domains and deployments for unauthorized changes using 'surge list' and verify content integrity of each site, rotate all authentication credentials including generating new Surge token via 'surge login' and updating all legitimate deployment systems, review git commit history for token exposure using 'git log -S "your-token"' and consider repository history rewriting if token committed, scan codebase for additional hardcoded secrets using automated secrets scanning tools, implement comprehensive secrets management strategy using dedicated tools (AWS Secrets Manager HashiCorp Vault Azure Key Vault Google Secret Manager) storing tokens encrypted at rest with access logging, configure CI/CD secure variable injection for deployment pipelines (GitHub Actions encrypted secrets GitLab CI/CD masked variables CircleCI contexts Jenkins credentials binding), enable deployment monitoring and alerts for Surge activity, implement pre-commit hooks to prevent credential commits using tools like git-secrets or detect-secrets, use .gitignore for files containing credentials (.npmrc .yarnrc .env surge-config.json), enable two-factor authentication on email account associated with Surge (tokens tied to email authentication), maintain detailed audit logs of all deployment operations, implement least-privilege access with separate tokens per environment (staging vs production), establish regular token rotation policy (quarterly or after team member departures), conduct security training for development teams on credential management best practices, and implement deployment approval workflows requiring manual verification for production changes. Additional security measures include: using Surge domain verification to prevent unauthorized domain claims, implementing Content Security Policy headers on deployed sites, enabling HTTPS and HSTS for all domains, monitoring Surge quota and billing for unexpected activity indicating abuse, maintaining backups of deployed content for rapid recovery, using separate Surge accounts for different projects to limit blast radius, implementing geo-blocking or IP allowlisting where appropriate, and establishing incident response procedures for credential exposure. Never commit Surge tokens to version control systems, share via insecure channels (Slack email), store in plaintext configuration files tracked by Git, reuse tokens across multiple projects or environments, embed in client-side code or public repositories, log tokens during application runtime, or include in error messages or debug output. Always use environment variables loaded at runtime, store in dedicated secrets management platforms, implement automated token rotation, separate tokens by environment and project, use minimal-scope credentials per deployment context, monitor token usage for suspicious activity, revoke tokens immediately upon suspected compromise, maintain detailed audit trails of credential access, implement alerts for new domain registrations or deployments, and conduct regular security reviews of Surge account activity and domain configurations. For secure Surge deployments: store token in system environment variables outside repository, use secrets managers in CI/CD workflows, implement deployment approval gates for production, maintain separate staging and production tokens, enable activity monitoring and alerting, regularly audit deployed content integrity, implement automated security scanning, maintain incident response playbooks, conduct periodic security assessments, and ensure team training on secure deployment practices.
